stories.headline: Israel and Lebanon agree to conditional ceasefire as Iran-war diplomacy continues
stories.body: Israel and Lebanon agreed in US-mediated talks to renew a conditional ceasefire and create pilot security zones in southern Lebanon where the Lebanese Armed Forces would take exclusive control and Hezbollah operatives would be barred. The agreement depends on Hezbollah halting attacks and withdrawing fighters from the area south of the Litani River, but Hezbollah was not part of the negotiations and fighting continued after the announcement, with Israeli strikes reported in southern Lebanon and Hezbollah saying it targeted Israeli soldiers. Israel’s defence minister said Israeli forces would remain in southern Lebanon for now and retain US-backed freedom to strike if Israeli communities are attacked, leaving the deal fragile as Washington tries to separate the Lebanon track from broader diplomacy over the Iran war.

stories.headline: Fighting in Mogadishu escalates amid dispute over delayed elections
stories.body: Government forces and opposition-allied militias exchanged heavy fire in Mogadishu after President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud’s term expired and parliament-backed constitutional changes opened the way for a one-year extension and delayed elections. Opposition figures, including former president Sharif Sheikh Ahmed and former prime minister Hassan Ali Khaire, accused government troops of attacking their homes and trying to suppress planned demonstrations, while police said they were carrying out a security operation against armed militias that had launched mortar attacks. Residents reported mortars, burning buildings and civilians fleeing with children, and the US, UK, EU and regional bloc Igad urged restraint and dialogue as Somalia’s long-running political instability and al-Shabaab insurgency continue to strain the country.

stories.headline: Ebola response in DRC hampered by deadly rebel attacks as outbreak timeline widens
stories.body: Rebel attacks blamed on the Islamic State-linked Allied Democratic Forces have killed more than 30 people around Beni in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, disrupting the response to an Ebola outbreak that has recorded hundreds of cases across North Kivu, South Kivu and Ituri. Local officials said three confirmed Ebola patients fled treatment centres after attacks, while community leaders warned that displacement and fear could spread infections and make containment harder. The WHO said the outbreak may have begun as early as January, giving the virus a significant head start, and said insecurity, mistrust, low contact-tracing rates and blanket travel restrictions were hindering efforts to catch up.

stories.headline: Ukraine and Russia trade major attacks as Kyiv claims battlefield gains
stories.body: Russia launched heavy attacks on Kyiv this week, setting fires in residential and non-residential areas and prompting Ukrainian officials to urge residents to take air raid alerts seriously after warnings of a possible massive strike. Ukraine, meanwhile, struck targets in Crimea and energy and military sites around Saint Petersburg, with Moscow-installed authorities reporting deaths in Crimea and US officials warning of escalation risks. Ukrainian and independent battlefield assessments cited by Spanish media say Kyiv has regained more territory than Russia for two straight months, a limited but significant trend attributed partly to Ukraine’s expanded drone production and attacks on Russian logistics, energy infrastructure and occupied Crimea supply routes.

stories.headline: US House votes to curb Trump’s authority over Iran war
stories.body: The US House passed a war powers resolution, 215-208, seeking to force President Donald Trump to seek congressional authorization for further military action in Iran or withdraw US forces. Four Republicans joined Democrats in the first successful House vote this year to rein in Trump’s Iran war powers, a symbolic but politically significant rebuke after more than 90 days of conflict that began with US and Israeli strikes in February. The measure’s legal force is uncertain because it is a concurrent resolution and the White House argues it is unconstitutional, but supporters said it shows growing bipartisan concern over an open-ended Middle East war, rising fuel prices and the lack of a diplomatic settlement.

stories.headline: US immigration authorities separated dozens of children from parents for a second time, AP finds
stories.body: An Associated Press investigation found that dozens of children separated from their families under the first Trump administration have been separated again despite a legal settlement meant to keep such families together until 2031. The report describes cases in which parents were detained or deported during renewed immigration enforcement sweeps, forcing them to choose whether to leave children behind in the United States, and cites emails indicating officials sometimes moved forward even after discovering families were legally protected from removal. One family returned to Florida only after a federal judge found the government had acted illegally, while DHS said it complies with court orders and accused outside groups and judges of obstructing enforcement.

stories.headline: Trump administration moves to rewrite rules for US higher education after campus investigations
stories.body: After a year of investigations and funding threats aimed at individual universities, the Trump administration is shifting to broader federal rulemaking that could affect thousands of US colleges. Education Department officials say they are trying to “course correct” higher education after judges blocked major funding cuts at Harvard and UCLA and after many civil rights lawyers left the department. The proposed regulatory approach targets many of the same areas pursued through campus investigations, including diversity, equity and inclusion policies, transgender athletes, antisemitism and practices the administration describes as anti-white discrimination.

stories.headline: Spanish political scandal over Leire Díez grows as Guardia Civil director and PSOE figures face scrutiny
stories.body: New material from the Audiencia Nacional investigation into the Leire Díez case has intensified scrutiny of senior Guardia Civil and PSOE-linked figures, with investigators examining whether Díez sought to undermine corruption inquiries affecting the Socialist party and people close to Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez. El País and La Vanguardia reported that WhatsApp messages, agenda entries and phone data point to multiple contacts or meetings between Díez and Guardia Civil director Mercedes González, whom Díez described as someone she trusted, while UCO investigators also examined internal Guardia Civil inquiries into alleged leaks from units investigating the government’s circle. El Periódico reported that the UCO inferred from intercepted messages that Sánchez knew of Díez’s activity, though that conclusion is based on references in chats and the case remains under judicial investigation.

stories.headline: UCO searches Tubos Reunidos and seeks SEPI records in secret Leire case offshoot
stories.body: Guardia Civil UCO agents searched Tubos Reunidos sites in Bilbao and Amurrio and requested information from SEPI as part of a still-secret branch of the Leire Díez investigation overseen by Audiencia Nacional judge Santiago Pedraz. Investigators are examining possible links between the company’s pandemic-era public rescue, its debt to SEPI and alleged commissions or influence operations involving figures connected to the case, including references to a November 2024 meeting at PSOE headquarters with Santos Cerdán, Díez and company executives. RTVE and La Vanguardia reported that investigators are seeking documentation on financial operations that may have diverted money to the alleged network, while Tubos Reunidos’ rescue and subsequent efforts to delay debt repayments are now part of the inquiry.

stories.headline: Catalan teachers reject education pre-agreement and prepare new strikes
stories.body: Catalan public school teachers rejected a pre-agreement with the Education Department, with 65.1% of more than 60,000 participating teachers voting no and backing further strike action. The deal, signed by USTEC and Professors de Secundària, included staged salary improvements, more staff for inclusive education and new secondary-school posts, but many teachers said it did not go far enough to address what unions call an education emergency. USTEC said Friday’s strike would go ahead and called for renewed talks, CGT and La Intersindical urged wider mobilization and demanded Education Minister Esther Niubó’s dismissal, while Professors de Secundària said its own members had endorsed the pact and withdrew from Friday’s strike.

stories.headline: Barcelona to eliminate private shared-bike licences over misuse
stories.body: Barcelona Mayor Jaume Collboni said the city will stop renewing licences for private app-based shared-bike companies, effectively removing about 3,500 bikes from the streets by 2027. The city says the services have generated thousands of sanctions, removals and resident complaints over badly parked bicycles and misuse of public space, while only a small share of users are Barcelona residents. Collboni said the move is meant to protect public space and prioritize resident mobility, while traditional bike-rental shops will not be affected and the city plans to expand the municipal Bicing system.
